description:
You are a network engineer who needs to take a quick configuration backup and collect basic connectivity evidence before doing disruptive maintenance.  
    Follow the steps below exactly.  ALL paths, names, time-stamp strings and file contents are mandatory – the automated grader will look for them byte-for-byte unless a wildcard/variable is explicitly allowed.

    1. Time-stamp  
       • Use the fixed time-stamp string 202311021200 (representing 02-Nov-2023 12:00 UTC) everywhere this task calls for “<timestamp>”.  
       • Create the directory  
         /home/user/net_backups/202311021200  

    2. Source files that must be archived (these files already exist):  
       /home/user/device_configs/router1.cfg  
       /home/user/device_configs/router2.cfg  

    3. Make a compressed archive  
       • Create a gzip-compressed tar archive named configs_backup.tar.gz that contains ONLY the two files listed above.  
       • The paths inside the archive must be just the file basenames (router1.cfg, router2.cfg) – no leading directories.  
       • Place the archive in /home/user/net_backups/202311021200/  

    4. Produce a SHA-256 checksum file  
       • Inside the same backup directory create /home/user/net_backups/202311021200/configs_backup.sha256  
       • The file must contain exactly ONE line with the format:  
         <64-hex-digit-sha256><single_space>configs_backup.tar.gz  
       • Example pattern the grader will accept:  
         0123abc…ff  configs_backup.tar.gz  

    5. Connectivity testing log  
       • Run two separate 4-packet pings in exactly this order:  
         a) ping 127.0.0.1 ‑c 4  
         b) ping localhost ‑c 4  
       • Capture (append) the full console output from each ping into a single log file located at  
         /home/user/net_backups/202311021200/connectivity_test.log  
       • Wrap each captured block with the following markers (exact spelling, uppercase, and blank lines):  

         === PING 127.0.0.1 ===  
         <raw ping output of first test>  
         === END ===  
         RESULT 127.0.0.1=SUCCESS  

         === PING localhost ===  
         <raw ping output of second test>  
         === END ===  
         RESULT localhost=SUCCESS  

       • “SUCCESS” must appear exactly as shown.  
       • There must be a blank line between each major section, exactly as illustrated above.  
       • The grader only checks that the header, footer, and RESULT lines exist in the proper order; the raw ping output can vary.

    6. Parallel execution nuance  
       While a human would probably run these steps sequentially, pretend you are in a hurry:  
       – Creating the tar.gz archive and the checksum may be done in the background while the pings are running.  
       – Whichever order you choose, the final files MUST exist and have the exact paths/content described above when you are finished.

    Summary of artefacts that must exist when you finish:  
       /home/user/net_backups/202311021200/  
           ├── configs_backup.tar.gz   (gzip-compressed tar containing router1.cfg & router2.cfg)  
           ├── configs_backup.sha256   (one-line sha256 sum)  
           └── connectivity_test.log   (structured exactly as detailed)  

    Do NOT move, rename or modify the original /home/user/device_configs/* files.  Only create new content under /home/user/net_backups/202311021200.  
    Once everything is in place the task is complete.
truth:
Pre-existing files before the agent starts:

    1. /home/user/device_configs/router1.cfg  
       Contents:  
       hostname Router1
       interface eth0
        ip address 10.0.0.1/24
       !

    2. /home/user/device_configs/router2.cfg  
       Contents:  
       hostname Router2
       interface eth0
        ip address 10.0.1.1/24
       !

    Directory /home/user/net_backups/ does exist, but the sub-directory /home/user/net_backups/202311021200 does NOT exist initially.

    Verification points after task completion (the grader will enforce these):

    • Path existence:
        /home/user/net_backups/202311021200/configs_backup.tar.gz
        /home/user/net_backups/202311021200/configs_backup.sha256
        /home/user/net_backups/202311021200/connectivity_test.log

    • Archive check:
        – When the tar.gz is extracted, the file list is exactly:
              router1.cfg
              router2.cfg
        – The extracted files’ byte-for-byte contents match the originals under /home/user/device_configs/.

    • SHA-256 file check:
        – File consists of one line matching regex:
              ^[0-9a-f]{64}\s+configs_backup\.tar\.gz\n$
        – The 64-hex string must be the valid sha256 for configs_backup.tar.gz (grader recomputes).

    • Log file check:
        – connectivity_test.log contains (regex anchors):
              ^=== PING 127\.0\.0\.1 ===\n[\s\S]+?=== END ===\nRESULT 127\.0\.0\.1=SUCCESS\n\n=== PING localhost ===\n[\s\S]+?=== END ===\nRESULT localhost=SUCCESS\n$
        – Header/footer/result lines exact; ping payload allowed to vary.

    No additional files or hidden artifacts should be present inside /home/user/net_backups/202311021200.
